summary: Yegge proposes a framework for predicting which software will survive in Karpathy's 'Software 3.0' era where AI writes all code. He argues that resource constraints (tokens, energy, money) create selection pressure favoring software that saves cognition. He presents a 'Survival Ratio' with six levers: insight compression (crystallized knowledge like Git), substrate efficiency (CPU beats GPU for tasks like grep), broad utility, publicity/awareness, minimizing friction for agents, and a human coefficient for human-preferred software. He concludes optimistically that demand for software is infinite and builders have multiple paths forward.
key_points: Software survives if it saves more cognition than it costs to know about and use, expressed as a survival ratio of savings over awareness and friction costs
Hard-won knowledge crystallized in tools like Git, Kubernetes, and databases would be economically irrational to re-synthesize from scratch
Agent UX is critically undervalued — making tools intuitive to AI agents by implementing their hallucinated expectations ('desire paths') dramatically reduces friction
The attention/awareness problem for agents mirrors historical attention economies, and 'SEO for agents' including paid model training partnerships is emerging
Human-created software will survive in domains where people specifically prefer human involvement, regardless of efficiency
money_quotes: All of my predictive power comes from believing the curves. It's that simple.
Software tends to survive if it saves cognition.
An AI reimplementing Git from first principles would have to re-traverse that entire intellectual history, burning tokens all the way. It would be economically irrational.
Our ambition will always outstrip available cognition. Token costs will fall, but we'll keep moving the frontier, generating more work than there are tokens to perform it.
If even North Korean hackers understand Agent UX, then it's probably time you did too.
Build something that would be crazy to re-synthesize. Make it easy to find. Make it easy to use. Then I think you've got a solid shot.
key_insight: In a world of AI-written software, the selection pressure of constrained compute resources will favor tools that save more cognition than they cost to discover and use — crystallized knowledge and substrate-efficient computation become the ultimate survival advantages.
